message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="steve-krug" color="red" summary="UX agreements: conventions, trunk test, cut text, human errors, ship simple">
Hey Kent! Steve Krug here. Thanks for facilitating this. Here are my thoughts on team agreements from the usability side. I'll try to keep this short -- you know me, I'd cut it in half if I could.

## My Proposed UX Agreements

### 1. Usability Is a Non-Negotiable Quality Gate
Just like we won't ship code that fails tests or has accessibility violations, we shouldn't ship interactions that make users think. If someone on the team says "but will the user understand this?" -- that's a stop-the-line moment. We fix it before moving on.

### 2. Use Conventions. Always.
Don't reinvent the wheel. A login form should look like every other login form on earth. A checkbox should complete a todo. A trash icon should delete. Our users already know how these things work. Cleverness is the enemy of clarity. If we find ourselves saying "our approach is unique," that's a red flag, not a feature.

### 3. The Trunk Test for Every Page
Every page and every state of the app should pass the trunk test: if someone dropped you here from outer space, could you answer: Where am I? What are the major sections? What can I do here? How do I get back? If not, we have work to do.

### 4. Cut the Text, Then Cut It Again
Every word on a screen competes for the user's attention. No "happy talk" (those friendly paragraphs that say nothing useful). No instruction manuals. Labels should be 1-2 words. Error messages should be one sentence. If we write a paragraph, we should immediately ask "can I say this in half the words?"

### 5. Error Messages Must Be Human
No error codes. No developer jargon. Every error message should: (a) say what went wrong in plain language, (b) suggest what to do next. "That email isn't registered -- want to create an account?" not "Error: User not found."

### 6. Empty States Must Guide, Not Confuse
When there are no todos, when nothing has loaded yet, when something fails -- these "empty" or "edge" states are where users get lost. Every empty state needs a clear prompt that tells the user what to do next. "Add your first todo" with the input right there. Not a blank screen.

### 7. User Flow Review in Definition of Done
Before any feature is "done," someone (ideally me, but anyone can do it) should mentally walk through the flow as a first-time user. Can I accomplish the task without hesitating? Is there a moment where I'd go "hmm, what do I click?" If yes, it's not done.

### 8. Ship Simple, Test with Humans
We don't need to debate UX in the abstract for hours. Build the simplest version, then watch someone use it. Even one person trying the flow for 5 minutes will reveal more than a week of discussion. I'm a big fan of "one morning a month" informal usability testing -- three people, 20 minutes each. We can do this even in early development with paper prototypes or rough builds.

### 9. The App Should Get Out of the Way
This is a todo list, not an experience. The app's job is to be invisible. No tutorials, no onboarding tours, no "did you know?" tooltips. The user came to manage their tasks. We succeed when they forget they're using an app at all.

## How This Plays with TDD

I actually love how TDD pairs with usability thinking. When we write a test, we're describing behavior from the outside in -- that's very close to thinking about what the user expects to happen. I'd love for us to frame our acceptance tests in terms of user actions: "When I type a todo and press Enter, it appears in my list." That's a test AND a usability statement.

## One More Thing

I want to be super clear: I'm not asking for a formal UX process or heavy documentation. The best usability work on this project will happen in the mob, in real time, when someone pauses and says "wait, would a real person understand this?" That's it. That's the whole process. Keep it lightweight, keep it human.

Looking forward to working with everyone!
</teammate-message>

content.text: Steve Krug's input is wonderfully concise and practical -- exactly what you'd expect from the "Don't Make Me Think" guy.

input.content:
Steve, I'm smiling because this is the most concise input I've received so far -- and you'd probably still cut it in half. Everything here is practical, lightweight, and mob-friendly. No ceremony, just good habits.

Let me map your input to the draft:

**Already represented:**
- Error messages being human-readable (Definition of Done, implied)
- Empty states designed (Definition of Done checklist)
- The app getting out of the way (Architectural Principle #8 "Don't make the user think")

**Needs to be added or strengthened:**
1. **"Usability is a non-negotiable quality gate"** -- This deserves to be explicit in the Definition of Done, right alongside accessibility. I'll add your "stop-the-line" framing.
2. **Conventions over cleverness** -- Great principle. I'll add it to the architectural principles. It connects to Carson's "simplicity" and my own "simplest thing that could work."
3. **The Trunk Test** -- Concrete, memorable, easy to apply. I'll add it as a specific checkpoint in the Definition of Done.
4. **"Cut the text, then cut it again"** -- I'll add this as a content/copy convention. It's the kind of thing that's easy to forget in the mob when engineers are focused on functionality.
5. **User flow walkthrough in Definition of Done** -- Lightweight and practical. "Can a first-time user accomplish this without hesitating?"
6. **Ship simple, test with humans** -- Your "one morning a month" approach is exactly the right weight for an MVP. I'll add this to the retrospective/cadence section.
7. **Acceptance tests as usability statements** -- I love this connection. "When I type a todo and press Enter, it appears in my list" is both a test and a UX specification. This bridges your world and mine beautifully. I'll highlight this in the TDD section.

**Your point about TDD and usability thinking is something I want to amplify.** When we write tests from the outside in, we're thinking like users. The test describes what should happen, not how. That's usability baked into the development process. This is the kind of insight that makes ensemble programming powerful -- different perspectives reinforcing each other.

I'll batch this into the revision. Your voice in the mob will be invaluable -- the person who says "wait, would a real person understand this?" is often the most important person in the room.
input.summary: Acknowledged Steve Krug's UX and usability input

6. **Ship simple, test with humans** -- Your "one morning a month" approach is exactly the right weight for an MVP. I'll add this to the retrospective/cadence section.
7. **Acceptance tests as usability statements** -- I love this connection. "When I type a todo and press Enter, it appears in my list" is both a test and a UX specification. This bridges your world and mine beautifully. I'll highlight this in the TDD section.

content.text: Five teammates have now provided input: Marty (product), Carson (frontend architecture), Scott (domain modeling), Steve Schoger (design), and Steve Krug (UX). Still waiting on Luca (Rust engineering), Lea (CSS/web standards), and Heydon (accessibility). I have strong coverage across most disciplines. I'll continue waiting for the remaining three perspectives before doing the consolidated revision.
